title: Pericarditis
oneLiner: Inflammation of pericardial layers; causes pleuritic chest pain; can mimic MI, rarely leads to life-threatening tamponade or effusion.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cardiac tamponade:** Hypotension + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds (Beck's triad); bedside echo shows pericardial effusion with right ventricular collapse.
- **Acute STEMI:** ECG shows regional ST elevation (not diffuse); troponin rises >10x upper limit; treat as ACS until proven otherwise.
- **Myocarditis:** Chest pain + arrhythmia + heart failure (↑ troponin, diffuse ECG changes, echo shows global hypokinesis); can mimic pericarditis but requires early cardiology involvement.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sharp, pleuritic chest pain – worsened inspiration, supine position; relieved sitting forward.
  - May radiate to trapezius ridge (phrenic nerve irritation).
  - Often prodromal viral illness (fever, myalgia, cough).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Pericardial friction rub** – high-pitched scratching sound (to-and-fro), best heard left sternal edge, patient leaning forward (ephemeral).
  - Muffled heart sounds if large effusion.
  - Tachycardia, low-grade fever.
  - Signs of cardiac tamponade (see Killers) if severe.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute MI / unstable angina** – Regional ST elevation, troponin rise >5x, pain not positional.
  - *Splitter:* Diffuse concave ST elevation + PR depression (pericarditis) vs. regional convex ST elevation (MI).
- **Pleurisy / pneumonia** – Cough, sputum, focal chest signs, no PR depression on ECG.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – Acute breathlessness, pleuritic pain, tachycardia, raised D-dimer, CT pulmonary angiogram positive.
- **Aortic dissection** – Tearing chest/back pain, pulse deficit, widened mediastinum.
- **Costochondritis** – Tenderness on palpation of costochondral joints, ECG normal.
- **Pneumothorax** – Sudden pleuritic pain, hyperresonance, reduced breath sounds, tracheal shift if tension.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - 12-lead ECG – within minutes. Look for: diffuse concave ST elevation (lead I, II, aVF, V3-V6) + PR depression (especially aVR, V1). Rule out STEMI criteria.
  - **Echocardiogram (transthoracic)** – assess effusion size, signs of tamponade (right atrial/ventricular collapse, dilated IVC with >50% respiratory variation) – ideally within 1 hour if unstable.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR – inflammatory markers raised.
  - Troponin (high-sensitivity) – usually mild elevation (<5x ULN) if myocardial involvement; if >5x, suspect myopericarditis or MI.
  - U&E, LFT, coagulation – baseline.
  - Viral serology if recurrent/atypical (Coxsackie, EBV, CMV, HIV).
  - **Never miss:** ECG in all chest pain patients – omission is serious error. Also check echo if any sign of effusion.
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray – may show enlarged cardiac silhouette (>250ml effusion = "water bottle" shape), but normal CXR does not exclude.
  - CT chest if PE suspected or atypical presentation.
  - Cardiac MRI – gold standard for chronic/recurrent cases or suspected constrictive pericarditis.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** European Society of Cardiology (2015): at least 2 of: chest pain, pericardial rub, diffuse ST elevation/PR depression, pericardial effusion. Use to confirm clinical diagnosis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Rule out Killers:** ECG +/- troponin, urgent echocardiogram if haemodynamic compromise or large effusion.
  - **Haemodynamically unstable** (tamponade): call cardiology, prepare for pericardiocentesis (echo-guided), give IV fluid bolus (500ml-1000ml crystalloid) while awaiting drainage.
  - **Pain control:** High-dose NSAIDs (first-line) – ibuprofen 600-800mg TDS or naproxen 500mg BDS, with PPI cover (e.g., lansoprazole 30mg OD). Avoid if contraindicated.
  - **Stable patient:** admit to ward (Clinical Decision Unit or cardiology ward).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue NSAIDs + colchicine (0.5mg BDS, reduced if CrCl <30 or weight <70kg: 0.5mg OD). Duration: 3 months for first episode, 6 months for recurrent.
  - Consider oral amoxicillin +/- clarithromycin if purulent pericarditis suspected (rare, seek ID advice).
  - **Default orders (if admitted):** fluids – oral or IV crystalloid 1L/24h if no fluid restriction; VTE prophylaxis – enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if normal renal function); diet – normal; observations – 4-hourly NEWS2.
  - Monitor symptoms, listen for rub disappearance, check CRP daily.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Wean NSAIDs after 1-2 weeks then stop colchicine at 3 months.
  - Repeat echo at 4-6 weeks to confirm resolution of effusion.
  - If recurrent or refractory: consider low-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.2-0.5mg/kg/day) only after failure of NSAIDs + colchicine – avoid early steroid as it increases recurrence risk.
  - Manage underlying cause: if TB, HIV, autoimmune, or uraemic, treat accordingly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Ibuprofen 600-800mg PO TDS (max 2.4g/day)  
  - Colchicine 0.5mg PO BDS for 3 months  
  - PPI: lansoprazole 30mg PO OD (or omeprazole 20mg OD) for NSAID protection
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Naproxen 500mg PO BDS (if no contraindication)
  - Aspirin 750-1000mg PO TDS (if post-MI pericarditis, under cardiology advice)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in peptic ulcer, renal impairment (CrCl <30), aspirin allergy, bleeding risk.
  - Colchicine: caution with clarithromycin, statins (increased myopathy risk); avoid in severe liver/renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Colchicine: reduce dose if CrCl 10-30 (0.5mg OD); avoid if CrCl <10.
  - NSAIDs: avoid entirely if GFR <30; use lowest dose if GFR 30-60.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Colchicine – narrow therapeutic index; overdose causes multiorgan failure. Do not exceed 0.5mg BDS in normal renal function. Always check baseline renal function.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Cardiac tamponade** (5-10%) – urgency for pericardiocentesis.
  - **Myopericarditis** – myocardial involvement causing heart failure, arrhythmias.
  - **Recurrence** (15-30%) – most common complication.
  - **Constrictive pericarditis** (rare, <1%) – thickened, fibrotic pericardium causes diastolic heart failure.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent pericarditis (may require colchicine or steroid sparing agents).
  - Chronic constrictive pericarditis (requires pericardiectomy).
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent in viral/idiopathic – 70-90% recover within 1-2 weeks; recurrences common but not life-threatening.  
- Poor if tamponade, purulent, neoplastic, or constrictive – 1-year mortality up to 20% if underlying malignant cause.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always listen for a pericardial rub** – it is diagnostic and often transient; ask patient to lean forward.
- **Always get an ECG in chest pain** – diffuses ST elevation + PR depression is pattern; missed diagnosis leads to inappropriate thrombolysis.
- **Check for effusion with echo** even if haemodynamically stable – small effusions can become tamponade rapidly.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give thrombolytics** for diffuse ST elevation unless STEMI criteria met – can cause haemorrhagic pericarditis.
- **Do NOT give corticosteroids early** (unless connective tissue disease or TB) – increases recurrence rate.
- **Do NOT discharge without echo** – small effusions may progress silently; reassess in 1-2 weeks.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening chest pain or breathlessness.
  - Fever >38°C, rigors, or sweats.
  - New-onset dizziness, palpitations, or passing out.
  - Inability to tolerate oral NSAIDs.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology outpatient review in 4-6 weeks.
  - Repeat echo at that time to confirm resolution.
  - Advise to avoid vigorous exercise until pain-free and CRP normal (usually 1-2 weeks).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising trend.
  - Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluid bolus.
  - Signs of tamponade (pulsus paradoxus, elevated JVP, muffled hearts).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - New arrhythmia (atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia).
  - GCS <15 or confusion – consider cerebral hypoperfusion from tamponade.

rawMarkdown:
## Pericarditis
**One-liner:** Inflammation of pericardial layers; causes pleuritic chest pain; can mimic MI, rarely leads to life-threatening tamponade or effusion.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cardiac tamponade:** Hypotension + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds (Beck's triad); bedside echo shows pericardial effusion with right ventricular collapse.
- **Acute STEMI:** ECG shows regional ST elevation (not diffuse); troponin rises >10x upper limit; treat as ACS until proven otherwise.
- **Myocarditis:** Chest pain + arrhythmia + heart failure (↑ troponin, diffuse ECG changes, echo shows global hypokinesis); can mimic pericarditis but requires early cardiology involvement.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sharp, pleuritic chest pain – worsened inspiration, supine position; relieved sitting forward.
  - May radiate to trapezius ridge (phrenic nerve irritation).
  - Often prodromal viral illness (fever, myalgia, cough).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Pericardial friction rub** – high-pitched scratching sound (to-and-fro), best heard left sternal edge, patient leaning forward (ephemeral).
  - Muffled heart sounds if large effusion.
  - Tachycardia, low-grade fever.
  - Signs of cardiac tamponade (see Killers) if severe.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute MI / unstable angina** – Regional ST elevation, troponin rise >5x, pain not positional.
  - *Splitter:* Diffuse concave ST elevation + PR depression (pericarditis) vs. regional convex ST elevation (MI).
- **Pleurisy / pneumonia** – Cough, sputum, focal chest signs, no PR depression on ECG.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – Acute breathlessness, pleuritic pain, tachycardia, raised D-dimer, CT pulmonary angiogram positive.
- **Aortic dissection** – Tearing chest/back pain, pulse deficit, widened mediastinum.
- **Costochondritis** – Tenderness on palpation of costochondral joints, ECG normal.
- **Pneumothorax** – Sudden pleuritic pain, hyperresonance, reduced breath sounds, tracheal shift if tension.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - 12-lead ECG – within minutes. Look for: diffuse concave ST elevation (lead I, II, aVF, V3-V6) + PR depression (especially aVR, V1). Rule out STEMI criteria.
  - **Echocardiogram (transthoracic)** – assess effusion size, signs of tamponade (right atrial/ventricular collapse, dilated IVC with >50% respiratory variation) – ideally within 1 hour if unstable.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR – inflammatory markers raised.
  - Troponin (high-sensitivity) – usually mild elevation (<5x ULN) if myocardial involvement; if >5x, suspect myopericarditis or MI.
  - U&E, LFT, coagulation – baseline.
  - Viral serology if recurrent/atypical (Coxsackie, EBV, CMV, HIV).
  - **Never miss:** ECG in all chest pain patients – omission is serious error. Also check echo if any sign of effusion.
- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray – may show enlarged cardiac silhouette (>250ml effusion = "water bottle" shape), but normal CXR does not exclude.
  - CT chest if PE suspected or atypical presentation.
  - Cardiac MRI – gold standard for chronic/recurrent cases or suspected constrictive pericarditis.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** European Society of Cardiology (2015): at least 2 of: chest pain, pericardial rub, diffuse ST elevation/PR depression, pericardial effusion. Use to confirm clinical diagnosis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Rule out Killers:** ECG +/- troponin, urgent echocardiogram if haemodynamic compromise or large effusion.
  - **Haemodynamically unstable** (tamponade): call cardiology, prepare for pericardiocentesis (echo-guided), give IV fluid bolus (500ml-1000ml crystalloid) while awaiting drainage.
  - **Pain control:** High-dose NSAIDs (first-line) – ibuprofen 600-800mg TDS or naproxen 500mg BDS, with PPI cover (e.g., lansoprazole 30mg OD). Avoid if contraindicated.
  - **Stable patient:** admit to ward (Clinical Decision Unit or cardiology ward).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue NSAIDs + colchicine (0.5mg BDS, reduced if CrCl <30 or weight <70kg: 0.5mg OD). Duration: 3 months for first episode, 6 months for recurrent.
  - Consider oral amoxicillin +/- clarithromycin if purulent pericarditis suspected (rare, seek ID advice).
  - **Default orders (if admitted):** fluids – oral or IV crystalloid 1L/24h if no fluid restriction; VTE prophylaxis – enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if normal renal function); diet – normal; observations – 4-hourly NEWS2.
  - Monitor symptoms, listen for rub disappearance, check CRP daily.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Wean NSAIDs after 1-2 weeks then stop colchicine at 3 months.
  - Repeat echo at 4-6 weeks to confirm resolution of effusion.
  - If recurrent or refractory: consider low-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone 0.2-0.5mg/kg/day) only after failure of NSAIDs + colchicine – avoid early steroid as it increases recurrence risk.
  - Manage underlying cause: if TB, HIV, autoimmune, or uraemic, treat accordingly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Ibuprofen 600-800mg PO TDS (max 2.4g/day)  
  - Colchicine 0.5mg PO BDS for 3 months  
  - PPI: lansoprazole 30mg PO OD (or omeprazole 20mg OD) for NSAID protection
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Naproxen 500mg PO BDS (if no contraindication)
  - Aspirin 750-1000mg PO TDS (if post-MI pericarditis, under cardiology advice)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in peptic ulcer, renal impairment (CrCl <30), aspirin allergy, bleeding risk.
  - Colchicine: caution with clarithromycin, statins (increased myopathy risk); avoid in severe liver/renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Colchicine: reduce dose if CrCl 10-30 (0.5mg OD); avoid if CrCl <10.
  - NSAIDs: avoid entirely if GFR <30; use lowest dose if GFR 30-60.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Colchicine – narrow therapeutic index; overdose causes multiorgan failure. Do not exceed 0.5mg BDS in normal renal function. Always check baseline renal function.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Cardiac tamponade** (5-10%) – urgency for pericardiocentesis.
  - **Myopericarditis** – myocardial involvement causing heart failure, arrhythmias.
  - **Recurrence** (15-30%) – most common complication.
  - **Constrictive pericarditis** (rare, <1%) – thickened, fibrotic pericardium causes diastolic heart failure.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent pericarditis (may require colchicine or steroid sparing agents).
  - Chronic constrictive pericarditis (requires pericardiectomy).

### Prognosis
- Excellent in viral/idiopathic – 70-90% recover within 1-2 weeks; recurrences common but not life-threatening.  
- Poor if tamponade, purulent, neoplastic, or constrictive – 1-year mortality up to 20% if underlying malignant cause.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always listen for a pericardial rub** – it is diagnostic and often transient; ask patient to lean forward.
- **Always get an ECG in chest pain** – diffuses ST elevation + PR depression is pattern; missed diagnosis leads to inappropriate thrombolysis.
- **Check for effusion with echo** even if haemodynamically stable – small effusions can become tamponade rapidly.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give thrombolytics** for diffuse ST elevation unless STEMI criteria met – can cause haemorrhagic pericarditis.
- **Do NOT give corticosteroids early** (unless connective tissue disease or TB) – increases recurrence rate.
- **Do NOT discharge without echo** – small effusions may progress silently; reassess in 1-2 weeks.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening chest pain or breathlessness.
  - Fever >38°C, rigors, or sweats.
  - New-onset dizziness, palpitations, or passing out.
  - Inability to tolerate oral NSAIDs.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology outpatient review in 4-6 weeks.
  - Repeat echo at that time to confirm resolution.
  - Advise to avoid vigorous exercise until pain-free and CRP normal (usually 1-2 weeks).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising trend.
  - Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluid bolus.
  - Signs of tamponade (pulsus paradoxus, elevated JVP, muffled hearts).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - New arrhythmia (atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia).
  - GCS <15 or confusion – consider cerebral hypoperfusion from tamponade.
